hypothesis: Adaptive EWMA alpha (burst-sensitive boost) closes the 15-request BurstGPT SLA gap in Online SOTSS without regressing Azure goodput/$
verdict: NEGATIVE RESULT — no hyperparameter configuration produces a frontier improvement on both traces simultaneously

algorithm.name: Adaptive EWMA Online SOTSS
algorithm.mechanism: Boost EWMA alpha from 0.1 to burst_alpha when tick_mean > burst_threshold * ewma_val; hold for burst_cooldown_ticks ticks

azure_llm_2024.note: Adaptive EWMA never triggers on Azure LLM 2024 at any tested threshold — smooth workload keeps tick_mean < 2.0 * ewma_val universally

after_adaptive_ewma_best_config.note: threshold=2.0 never triggers on BurstGPT trace

after_adaptive_ewma_aggressive_config.note: threshold=1.5 triggers, recovers 4/15 requests, but over-provisions — goodput/$ regresses

root_cause_analysis.revised_diagnosis: The BurstGPT 15-request gap is a stochastic-vs-deterministic simulation mismatch, not an EWMA prediction error
root_cause_analysis.mechanism: The oracle's convergence check uses deterministic FIFO response times (no spot interruptions). The stochastic GSF evaluation includes Binomial interruptions (p_survive=0.9982/tick) that occasionally reduce effective capacity on heavy-load ticks, exposing borderline requests. EWMA predictions guide the oracle to the right ticks, but cannot compensate for the stochastic interruption buffer needed.
root_cause_analysis.why_adaptive_helps_partially: Adaptive EWMA overestimates service time on burst ticks → oracle adds MORE servers → covers some stochastic-interrupt buffer. But this is pure over-provisioning: adds servers to more ticks than needed → cost rises → goodput/$ falls.
root_cause_analysis.original_hypothesis_status: FALSIFIED. The gap is NOT caused by oracle fixing wrong ticks due to EWMA underestimation. Adaptive EWMA correctly identifies burst ticks but cannot improve the stochastic/deterministic mismatch without over-provisioning.
